text: The surgeon prepares for a laparoscopic right colectomy on a patient with a cecal tumor, marked by a visible tattoo. Three ports are used: a 5mm port in the left upper quadrant, a 10mm umbilical port for the camera, and a 12mm suprapubic port. The ileocolic pedicle is tented, and the mesentery folds are undone to expose the base of the pedicle. Medial to lateral dissection begins beneath the ileocolic pedicle, with the duodenum coming into view and swept down. The peritoneum is opened around the pedicle, revealing the artery and vein, which are isolated for secure ligation.

The surgeon prepares for a laparoscopic right colectomy on a patient with a cecal tumor, marked by a tattoo. Three ports are used: a 5mm in the left upper quadrant, a 10mm umbilical port for the camera due to an umbilical hernia, and a 12mm suprapubic port. The ileocolic pedicle is tented, and folds of the mesentery are undone to expose the base of the pedicle. Medial to lateral dissection begins beneath the ileocolic pedicle, with the duodenum swept down and the peritoneum opened around the pedicle. The artery and vein are isolated, with the iliacolic vein clipped before division.

The surgeon identifies bulky lymph nodes within the transected mesentery and continues medial dissection along the expected path of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV). The dissection proceeds underneath the mesentery along the visible white line, utilizing a bloodless plane that separates easily without sharp dissection.

The surgeon observes bulky lymph nodes in the transected mesentery and continues medial dissection along the expected path of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV). The dissection proceeds underneath the mesentery along the visible white line, which is a bloodless plane, allowing easy separation without sharp dissection. The head of the pancreas is visible as the surgeon thins out the mesentery to isolate the blood vessel.

The surgeon uses three laparoscopic ports to achieve adequate exposure and proceeds to dissect the mesentery to isolate the right colic vessels. The head of the pancreas is visible during the dissection. The surgeon clips and divides the artery and vein, ensuring minimal residual bleeding by removing surrounding fat tissue.

The surgeon clips and divides the artery and vein, ensuring high ligation for oncologic principles related to the cecal tumor. The medial dissection continues over the duodenum towards the hepatic flexure, revealing the right branch of the middle colic artery within the tissue. The planned colon margin is identified, and a clip is placed for ease of identification. The omentum is taken with the specimen as part of the right colectomy procedure.

The surgeon continues the medial dissection over the duodenum towards the hepatic flexure, identifying the right branch of the middle colic artery within a wad of tissue. The planned colon margin is marked with a clip for later identification. The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the planned colon margin, preparing it to be taken with the specimen.

The surgeon splits the omentum at the planned colon margin and proceeds with lateral dissection. The previous medial dissection over the pancreas and duodenum simplifies this step, allowing the surgeon to use a ligature for faster dissection. The surgeon completes the lateral attachments from an inferior angle and then transitions to the small bowel mesentery, unfolding it for easier transection.

The surgeon proceeds with lateral dissection, having completed a full medial dissection over the pancreas and duodenum. The lateral dissection is facilitated by the prior medial dissection, requiring only a few cell layers of tissue to be separated. The surgeon uses a ligature device to detach the cecal attachments, approaching the remaining lateral attachments from an inferior angle.

The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization of the cecum and terminal ileum, unfolding the mesentery for easier transection. The small bowel mesentery is then transected, with a perfusion assessment conducted to ensure adequate blood flow before using a stapler to transect the bowel. The stapling is performed through the suprapubic port, which will be upsized for specimen extraction.

The surgeon prepares to transect the bowel after completing medial and lateral attachments. A perfusion assessment is performed using ICG mode to ensure adequate blood flow at the planned transection sites. The stapler is aligned with the bowel and the colon is transected, followed by the small bowel. All stapling is conducted through the suprapubic port, which will be upsized for specimen extraction.

The surgeon places the divided specimen into the pelvis and secures a stay suture on the stapled end of the colon to the small bowel, approximately seven centimeters proximal. Using a suture passer, the surgeon stabilizes the isoparastaltic anastomosis. A colotomy is made with a Maryland instrument, followed by an enterotomy, ensuring not to penetrate the back wall of the bowel. The surgeon staples through the suprapubic port using a vascular load stapler for the common channel.

The surgeon places the divided specimen into the pelvis and secures a stay suture on the stapled end of the colon to the small bowel, approximately seven centimeters proximal. Using a suture passer, the surgeon stabilizes the isoparastaltic anastomosis. A colotomy is made with a Maryland instrument, followed by an enterotomy, ensuring not to penetrate the back wall of the bowel. The surgeon staples through the suprapubic port using a vascular load stapler for the common channel.

The surgeon aligns the colon and small bowel to minimize the size of the colotomy and enterotomy. Using a barbed suture, the surgeon begins closure at the near corner, employing a baseball stitch technique to ensure a secure closure. The suture is cinched after each bite to maintain tension, with a transition stitch applied as the surgeon sews away from themselves.

The surgeon aligns the colon and small bowel to minimize the size of the colotomy and common channel enterotomy. Using a barbed suture, the surgeon begins closure at the near corner, employing a baseball stitch technique. Each suture bite is cinched to ensure secure closure, with a transition stitch applied at the near corner. The surgeon uses a nine-inch V-lock suture, maintaining tension throughout the process, and plans to add a second layer using a Lembert stitch.

The surgeon uses a nine-inch V-lock suture to perform a Lembert stitch, ensuring proper spacing and tension. The suture is applied to bury the first layer, with the surgeon checking the near corner for complete closure. Clips are applied to secure the suture before cutting the tethering, concluding the operation.

The surgeon completes the suturing of the anastomosis, ensuring the first layer is buried and secure. They check the near corner for complete closure, occasionally using a silk suture as a target. Clips are applied to secure the V-lock suture before cutting the suture tethering, concluding the operation.
